comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the concept of autoresearch, particularly its implementation as an automated hyperparameter tuning and bug fixing loop using LLMs. While some commenters appreciate its utility in automating repetitive tasks and incremental improvements, others critique it as mostly trial and error with limited innovation and question its cost-effectiveness and broader applicability.

comments.author: n_bhavikatti
comments.text: The temperature clamp fix and "Optuna++" actions by the agents (the cause of basically all improvement to eCLIP) indicate they are good at finding bugs and hyper-parameter tuning. But when it comes to anything beyond that, such as novel architectural shifts, agents aren't good enough. With no clear path forward they tend to randomly change things, which is a poor approach. Agents: Optimization >> innovation
comments.analysis: This comment accurately distinguishes between optimization capabilities and innovation limitations of autoresearch agents, providing a nuanced view of their strengths and weaknesses.

comments.author: _pdp_
comments.text:
Take some working code. Ask an LLM to fix bugs. Measure performance and test coverage. Feed the results back into the LLM. Repeat.

This has been the standard approach for more complex LLM deployments for a while now in our shop.

Using different models across iterations is also something I've found useful in my own experiments. It's like getting a fresh pair of eyes.
comments.analysis: This comment succinctly describes a practical and common approach to using LLMs in iterative code improvement, aligning well with the autoresearch concept discussed.

comments.author: carlsborg
comments.text:
> “ The agent acted like a hyperparameter optimization algorithm with some basic reasoning baked in.”

Good lens.

The crux of the auto research repo is basically one file - program.md which is a system prompt that can be summarized as “do this in a loop: improve train.py, run the training, run evals, record result. Favor simplicity”. The other files are an arbitrary ML model that is being trained.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a clear and accurate summary of the autoresearch implementation, emphasizing its simplicity and iterative nature.

comments.author: lucasay
comments.text: This feels less like automated research and more like structured trial and error with a decent feedback loop. Still useful, but I think the real bottleneck is how good your eval metric is. If that’s weak, the whole loop just optimizes for the wrong thing faster.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully critiques the approach by highlighting the importance of evaluation metrics, which is a critical factor in automated research loops.

comments.author: dvt
comments.text:
Ok, so looking at the commit log[1], I was mostly interested in seeing what the "moonshot ideas" implementations looked like, but basically everything is just hyperparameter tuning. Which is nice, but likely not worth the $$$ spent on the tokens. Am I missing something here?

[1] https://github.com/ykumards/eCLIP/commits/main/autoresearch
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid concern about cost-effectiveness and the limited scope of the current autoresearch implementation focused on hyperparameter tuning.

comments.author: Xx_crazy420_xX
comments.text:
Autoresearch is nothing new, big players are already in the game with more sophisticated solutions:

  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.02660 (MARS)
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.14525 (Execution-grounded automated AI research)
  - https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.10402 (ML-Master 2.0)
The mostly used benchmark for automated AI engineering/ research is: https://github.com/openai/mle-bench
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful context by referencing existing research and benchmarks, situating the discussed project within the broader field.

comments.author: love2read
comments.text: So... It did work. It found bugs (that he didn't know about) and it did optimization (that he hadn't done).
comments.analysis: This comment confirms the practical success of the approach in bug finding and optimization, supporting the project's effectiveness in certain tasks.

comments.author: jpcompartir
comments.text:
There are better techniques for hyper-parameter optimisation, right? I fear I have missed something important, why has Autoresearch blown up so much?

The bottleneck in AI/ML/DL is always data (volume & quality) or compute.

Does/can Autoresearch help improve large-scale datasets? Is it more compute efficien than humans?
comments.analysis: The comment questions the novelty and impact of autoresearch compared to existing hyperparameter optimization methods, reflecting a common skepticism.

comments.author: pikachu0625
comments.text: It's better to outsource optimization phases. Our idea should be for constraint, assumptions etc. for breakthrough. Boyd often argues that once you can express a problem in a standard mathematical form, the implementation becomes a commodity that software can handle automatically.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a strategic perspective emphasizing human-led problem formulation over automated optimization, highlighting a limitation of current autoresearch.

comments.author: lamroger
comments.text:
Awesome breakdown! It really feels like a hyper-hyper parameter search + bug fixer.

I started looking at Kaggle again and autoresearch seems to converge to many of the solution vibes there.

Wild ensembles, squeezing a bit of loss out. More engineering than research IMO
comments.analysis: This comment captures the general sentiment that autoresearch is more about engineering iterative improvements than true research innovation.
